---
title: "QCRC Report Checklist and Rubric"
format: html
editor: visual
---

# Minimum viable report checklist

A minimum viable report is short, reproducible, and interpretable. It does not
need to be fancy.

Core requirements:

- The report renders without errors.
- The title names the dataset or topic and the main outcome.
- The Introduction states a research question and hypothesis.
- The Methods identify the dataset, sheet or file, variables, cleaning steps,
  and statistical analysis.
- The Results include at least one descriptive table or figure.
- The Results include at least one model or formal test aligned with the
  question.
- The Discussion gives the main interpretation, one limitation, and one next
  step.
- Code chunks have useful labels and avoid dumping unnecessary output.
- Claims use cautious language when the analysis is associational.

# Exercise 1 rubric

Use Exercise 1 to create a solid draft. "Meets expectations" is enough for the
end of Day 1.

| Area | Core learning goal | Meets expectations | Stretch goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quarto structure | Start a manuscript-style document | Report has title, sections, runnable chunks, and a successful render | Uses captions, cross-references, or alternate output formats |
| Data loading | Load and inspect QCRC or justified alternate data | Reads QCRC `Main_Dataset`, cleans names, checks rows/columns and missingness | Reads all QCRC sheets and summarizes workbook structure |
| Statistics | Connect a question to an appropriate first-pass analysis | Includes one descriptive table/figure and one test or model | Adds a sensitivity analysis or better variable coding |
| Iteration/functions | Use Day 1 programming habits | Avoids repeated copy-paste where a simple object or function would help | Writes a helper function for repeated summaries |
| Interpretation | Draft realistic prose for adult professional readers | States a cautious interpretation and at least one limitation | Clearly separates clinical, public health, and statistical significance |

# Exercise 2 rubric

Use Exercise 2 to turn the draft into a polished deliverable.

| Area | Core learning goal | Meets expectations | Stretch goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuity | Continue the Exercise 1 manuscript arc | Starts from the Exercise 1 draft and improves it | Shows a short change log from draft to final |
| Reusable data workflow | Make loading and cleaning reproducible | Defines and uses a data-loading function | Separates helper functions into a sourced script |
| Functional programming | Use repeated logic intentionally | Uses `lapply()` or similar logic for a workbook inventory | Reads selected non-main sheets and documents a conservative join |
| Formulas and S3 | Fit and inspect model objects | Fits crude and adjusted models with formula syntax and uses `summary()`, `coef()`, or `confint.default()` | Uses `predict()`, `anova()`, model comparison, or marginal predictions |
| Reporting | Produce a professional Quarto deliverable | Includes polished sections, captions, model table, figure, and limitation | Adds bibliography, appendix diagnostics, or non-HTML output |

# Optional advanced goals

Advanced work should deepen the same report instead of creating a second
unrelated analysis.

- Compare models with AIC or likelihood ratio tests.
- Estimate absolute risks or risk differences from model predictions.
- Add a sensitivity analysis for a different mortality endpoint.
- Use a non-main QCRC sheet, with careful documentation of row level and join
  assumptions.
- Add a bibliography and cite relevant clinical or epidemiologic literature.
- Bootstrap a coefficient or predicted estimate and compare uncertainty
  intervals.

# Instructor review notes

Review this rubric before teaching:

- Confirm whether QCRC variable coding documentation should be added.
- Confirm whether the treatment variable labels are correct for the current
  workbook.
- Decide whether solution documents should be distributed before, during, or
  after class.
